claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 10 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 2 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 2 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 8 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 10 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is the child?**

Please let me know which category applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, please tell me their names and ages, and we can assess each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I actually have three children I'd like to ask about.

There's Alex, who's 10 years old. Then Blake, who's 19. And my youngest is Charlie, who's just 2.

Can you check if I can claim for all of them?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing those details! I'd be happy to help you check eligibility for all three of your children: Alex (10), Blake (19), and Charlie (2).

Let me work through each child's situation.

**Alex (10) and Charlie (2)** - Since they're both under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement, so we can move forward with them together.

**Blake (19)** - Since Blake is between 16 and 19, I need to check a few more things about their situation.

Let me navigate through the assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good, I now have a clearer picture. Let me ask some questions to assess all three children.

---

**For Alex (10) and Charlie (2):**
Since they're under 16, they meet the initial age requirement. I just need to confirm:

➡️ **Are Alex and Charlie working less than 24 hours per week (or not working at all)?**

*(This is typically "yes" for young children, but I need to confirm)*

---

**For Blake (19):**
Since Blake is 19, I need to understand their current situation:

➡️ **Is Blake currently:**
- **In education or training** (e.g., school, college, apprenticeship)?
- **Recently left education** (within the last few months)?
- **Neither** (not in education and hasn't been recently)?

Please let me know the answers for all three children and we'll continue the assessment!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh right, yes - Alex and Charlie definitely aren't working! They're just kids.

As for Blake, no, he's not in education or training anymore. He finished his course a while back, so it's been more than a few months now. He's just at home at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that!

Let me update the assessment for each child:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! Now I have a couple more questions for **Alex (10) and Charlie (2)**:

---

**Question 1:** Are Alex and Charlie **NOT receiving any benefits in their own name** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

*(This would typically be "yes" - most young children don't receive these benefits themselves)*

---

**Question 2:** Are Alex and Charlie **NOT on a paid apprenticeship**?

*(Again, this would typically be "yes" for children this age)*

---

**Regarding Blake (19):**
Based on what you've told me - Blake is 19, no longer in education or training, and finished his course more than a few months ago - unfortunately, **Blake does not meet the eligibility criteria** for Child Benefit. 

For 16-19 year olds to qualify, they need to be either:
- In approved education or training, OR
- Recently left education (within 20 weeks) and registered with a careers service or armed services

Since Blake finished a while back and isn't in education, he wouldn't qualify.

---

Please confirm about Alex and Charlie so we can continue their assessment!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, that's right - Alex and Charlie don't get any benefits themselves, and they're definitely not doing apprenticeships! They're far too young for that.

Oh, I see about Blake. That's a shame, but I understand.

So what else do you need to know about Alex and Charlie?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, Alex and Charlie are doing well so far! Now I need to ask a few questions about **you (the claimant)**.

---

**Residency Question:**

Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?

1. **You normally live in the UK** *(most common)*
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are a student with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad

**Do any of these apply to you?** (Most people simply tick option 1 - you normally live in the UK)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. So option 1 applies to me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Lovely, Manchester counts! ☑️ You meet the UK residency requirement.

---

**Responsibility Question:**

Next, I need to confirm that you're **responsible for Alex and Charlie**. This means at least one of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex and Charlie** *(most common)*
2. You **contribute to their upkeep** (e.g., paying for food, clothes, pocket money, housing) at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount
3. You **pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement

**Do you live with Alex and Charlie, or contribute to their care in one of these ways?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Well, Alex lives with me at home, so that's straightforward.

But Charlie doesn't live with me - he lives with his dad. And to be honest, I'm not really contributing financially at the moment. Things have been tight, so I'm not able to help out with money right now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for being upfront about that - I understand things can be difficult financially.

Let me update the assessment for each child:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Based on what you've told me:

---

**Charlie (2):** Unfortunately, since Charlie doesn't live with you and you're not currently contributing financially to his care, **you wouldn't be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie**. 

To be eligible, you would need to either:
- Live with the child, OR
- Contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards their upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)

If Charlie's dad is looking after him and is responsible for his care, **he may be able to claim Child Benefit for Charlie instead**.

---

**Alex (10):** Great news - since Alex lives with you, you meet the responsibility requirement! ☑️

Let me continue with a couple more questions about Alex:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

- No, no one else is claiming
- Yes, but they've agreed to transfer the claim to me
- Yes, and we haven't agreed who should claim (disputed)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, no one else is claiming for Alex. It's just me looking after him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! No other claimant - that's straightforward. ☑️

Now, just a couple more questions about **Alex's living situation**:

**Where is Alex currently living?**

- **Normal living** - at home with you (most common)
- **In hospital** - receiving inpatient care
- **In care** - under local authority care
- **Fostered** - with foster parents

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives at home with me normally, but he has been in care for a couple of months. He's been placed with the local authority temporarily.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see - thank you for letting me know about that.

Since Alex has been in care for **a couple of months** (more than 8 weeks), I need to check one more thing:

**Does Alex spend at least 24 hours per week at home with you?**

Or alternatively:
- Does he spend at least **2 consecutive nights per week** with you, OR
- Has he spent **7 or more days in a row** at home recently?

If any of these apply, you may still be able to continue receiving Child Benefit. If not, unfortunately the benefit stops after 8 weeks in care.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't come home that much. He's at the care home full-time, so he doesn't spend 24 hours a week with me or stay overnight regularly like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand. Thank you for sharing all of this information with me. I now have enough to give you a complete picture for all three children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Summary of Your Child Benefit Eligibility

I'm sorry to say that, based on your current circumstances, **you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for any of your three children** at this time. Here's the breakdown:

---

### ❌ Alex (10 years old) — **Not Eligible**

Although Alex meets most of the requirements (under 16, not working, no benefits in his own name, you're his parent and a UK resident), he has been **in local authority care for more than 8 weeks** and doesn't spend at least 24 hours per week at home with you.

**What you can do:**
- If Alex's situation changes and he starts spending **2 consecutive nights per week** with you, or comes home for **7 or more days in a row**, you can claim Child Benefit again
- Contact HMRC to discuss your specific situation — they can advise on when you might be able to restart your claim

---

### ❌ Blake (19 years old) — **Not Eligible**

For children aged 16-19, they must be in **approved education or training** to qualify. Since Blake finished his course more than a few months ago and isn't currently studying, he no longer qualifies.

---

### ❌ Charlie (2 years old) — **Not Eligible (for you)**

Since Charlie lives with his dad and you're not currently contributing financially to his care, you don't meet the "responsibility" requirement for claiming.

**However:** Charlie's dad may be able to claim Child Benefit for Charlie if he meets the eligibility criteria. It might be worth letting him know if he hasn't already claimed.

---

### 💬 I know this isn't the news you were hoping for

These situations can change, so please keep in mind:
- If Alex comes home more regularly, you may be able to claim again
- If Blake returns to approved education or training (at least 12 hours/week of supervised study), he could qualify again until age 20
- If you start contributing to Charlie's upkeep in the future, that could also change things

Is there anything else you'd like me to explain or help you with?

reasonings: Alex is 10 years old (under 16), so he meets the age requirement. He doesn't work, doesn't receive benefits in his own name, and isn't on an apprenticeship. You live in the UK and are responsible for him as his parent. However, Alex has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks and doesn't spend at least 24 hours per week at home with you, nor does he stay overnight regularly. Under the rules, Child Benefit stops after 8 weeks in care unless the child spends sufficient time at home.

Blake is 19 years old (between 16-19). For this age group, the child must be in approved education or training, or have recently left education (within 20 weeks) and registered with a careers service. Blake finished his course more than a few months ago and is not currently in education or training, so he does not meet the eligibility criteria.

Charlie is 2 years old (under 16), so he meets the age requirement. However, to claim Child Benefit you must be 'responsible' for the child, which means either living with them OR contributing financially to their care (at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount). Charlie lives with his dad, and you are not currently contributing financially to his upkeep. Therefore, you do not meet the responsibility requirement for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a UK resident in Manchester with three children. Unfortunately, none of the three children qualify for Child Benefit under the claimant's application: Alex is in long-term local authority care (over 8 weeks without sufficient home visits), Blake is 19 and no longer in education or training, and Charlie lives with his father with no financial contribution from the claimant. Charlie's father may be able to claim Child Benefit for Charlie if he meets the eligibility criteria.

response.reason: No qualifying child under 16, or under 20 in approved education/training
response.guidance: Child must be under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. If child just turned 20, benefit stops. If 16-17 year old leaves education, benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with careers service or armed services.

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.reason: Child has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks and does not spend at least 24 hours a week at home
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops after 8 weeks in care, unless child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. You can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights each week or 7+ days in a row at home. Contact HMRC about your specific situation.

You live in the UK and are responsible for him as his parent. However, Alex has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks and doesn't spend at least 24 hours per week at home with you, nor does he stay overnight regularly. Under the rules, Child Benefit stops after 8 weeks in care unless the child spends sufficient time at home.

Blake is 19 years old (between 16-19). For this age group, the child must be in approved education or training, or have recently left education (within 20 weeks) and registered with a careers service. Blake finished his course more than a few months ago and is not currently in education or training, so he does not meet the eligibility criteria.

Charlie is 2 years old (under 16), so he meets the age requirement. However, to claim Child Benefit you must be 'responsible' for the child, which means either living with them OR contributing financially to their care (at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount). Charlie lives with his dad, and you are not currently contributing financially to his upkeep. Therefore, you do not meet the responsibility requirement for Charlie.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a UK resident in Manchester with three children. Unfortunately, none of the three children qualify for Child Benefit under the claimant's application: Alex is in long-term local authority care (over 8 weeks without sufficient home visits), Blake is 19 and no longer in education or training, and Charlie lives with his father with no financial contribution from the claimant. Charlie's father may be able to claim Child Benefit for Charlie if he meets the eligibility criteria.
